mnemonics.title: GREEN IT Pillars
mnemonics.content: Remember the triple bottom line with: 'PEP' = Planet, Environment, Profit - think of it as People (Social), Environment (Environmental), and Profit (Economic)

mnemonics.title: PUE Formula
mnemonics.content: PUE = 'Power Used Everywhere' - Total Facility Energy ÷ IT Equipment Energy. Lower is better, 1.0 is perfect!

mnemonics.title: E-Waste Hazards
mnemonics.content: Remember toxic e-waste materials with: 'Lead Mercury Cadmium Chromium' = LMCC (Like Many Contaminated Computers)

mnemonics.title: Green IT Benefits
mnemonics.content: C-E-R: Cost savings, Environmental protection, Reputation improvement

acronyms.usage: Metric to measure data center energy efficiency - lower values indicate better efficiency
Discarded electronic devices requiring special disposal methods
Organisations' obligation to consider environmental and social impacts
Includes purchase cost, energy consumption, and disposal cost for IT assets
Emissions from IT operations contributing to climate change

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a pie chart where IT Equipment Energy is the 'computing slice' and supporting energy (cooling, lighting) is the 'wasted slice'. Smaller wasted slice = better PUE!
Picture one physical server as a 'multi-story building' with multiple 'tenants' (virtual machines) sharing the same foundation (hardware).
Think of a boomerang: IT products go from manufacturing → use → disposal → recycling → back to manufacturing. The goal is to make it a closed loop!
Imagine an iceberg where the tip (visible) is manufacturing emissions and the massive underwater portion is energy consumption during use. Both need attention!

analogies.analogy: Just as a single school building hosts multiple classes, one physical server can run multiple virtual computers efficiently.
Like a 'green building' with solar panels and natural ventilation, a green data center uses renewable energy and natural cooling.
E-waste is like a 'time bomb' - if not handled properly, toxic materials leak into environment just like radioactive materials.
Energy-efficient IT is like a 'hybrid car' - gets the same output (transportation/computing) while using less fuel (energy).
